text: So far I am really digging it! PLA was more focused around catching, whereas this one feels more battle oriented. The new battle system isn’t perfect, but it definitely is a nice switch up from the mainline games. I do find myself missing the turn based combat, though. What I really do like a lot though is I find I’m using Pokémon that I wouldn’t normally use. Type matchups are important, but with the new battle system using tackle, quick attack, and swift is completely viable and you’re able to win using whatever as long as you’re same level. The city is small, there’s no denying that. It feels bing a fresh for the first couple hours, but then you realize you’ve seen everything once you’ve walked around for ~10-15 minutes. The wild zones are small and you can catch everything quickly.Even with my complaints of the city being small and samey, I’m having A LOT of fun with A-Z. Hearing all the random stuff the townsfolk say really brings the world to life. The gameplay loop is addictive. I look forward to nightfall every day so I can battle and move up the ranks and when day time hits I run around doing all the side quests that flesh out every day life in the world of Pokémon. I say pick it up if you’re a Pokémon fan!

text: One thing I will note is that this game builds on a lot of changes made in Legends Arceus. That game was a big shakeup in terms of entrenched systems and mechanics, and the Legends sub-franchise seems to be rebuilding the games from the ground up. While I hope some of the specific decisions made for this game don't propagate forward into other games, I think the overall design process they are following for the Legends games order to break out of the shell of previous games and not make the same mistakes S/V did is the right path. Specifics:-I prefer this one over Arceus. The plot is better, the dialogue is better, the characters are better written, and the interface is, for the most part, easier to use. -Getting used to real-time battles can be a lot. If you're using a slow pokemon with slow moves, it can be frustrating to have the game accept the command, but then get K.O.ed by someone faster without having your move go off. It can also be frustrating to K.O. an enemy pokemon and still have their move go off as they faint. -The new positioning can be frustrating with the way commands are executed and pokemon follow you. Moving during battle = repositioning your pokemon. If you're moving around while your pokemon is making an attacks, and then have it execute a ranged attack, it will walk to your new position and execute the ranged attack from there even if the enemy was already in range. -Trying swap active pokemon or open the menu to use an item and having the interface not respond because your pokemon is still in an animation or it's on cooldown without having any indicators displaying that it's on cooldown or for how long can be frustrating. -Once you're used to the real-time it can be a lot of fun. Moves have different execution times and different cooldowns. Not only do moves like Quick Attack and Aqua Jet have low cooldowns, their animations are quick as well. This means you can use them sooner after using another move, and use another move more quickly after using them. -Live positioning also opens up a lot of options once you're used to the cognitive load of having to think about it. If you are moving and haven't given your pokemon an attack command, it will follow you. Faster pokemon will follow you more quickly. You can have them physically dodge AoE attacks like Rock Throw or Hydro Pump, or slower attacks like Dig and Bounce. Run away and then use a ranged attack on them while they close the distance to hit you with Crunch. And if they try the same thing on you, moves like Headbutt and Quick Attack have your pokemon dash to their target from a distance away.

text: I LOVE the combat mechanic. Sure, one could argue that turn-based is better for tournaments/important fights, but for regular farming and catching this mechanic is superior by a mile. The boss battle is the best Pokemon has ever had and clearly improved on Arceus' formula. The character designs are top-notch - I can't single out any character who has shitty/weird design. The side quests and dialogues are probably the best standouts from previous titles, though, and breathe more life to an otherwise inadequate overworld. So yea, there are plenty to love about ZA, because I unironically think that the aspects it executed well are the best in the franchise. However, I know the bad rap is coming from. Hyperspaces look like crap with atrocious pathing. The special hyperspaces of the Weather Trio and Darkrai look amazing but so underutilized - I wish they would have reused those assets more. I'm generally cool with grinding but I can't stand the forced RNG in the DLC with the donuts and special scans, so I ended up never getting the Porygon hyperspace before moving on to Pokopia. And no, the pros of ZA still does not put it in the same league with Arceus at all. And I did a full Arceus story playthrough between Darkrai and Rayquaza.

text: I think it depends on the approach. As someone who likes to 100% stuff, the games mechanics and flow made it so that I didn't actually enjoy them at their best. I understand that I made the decision to play a certain way and that effects how I feel about the game, but the game put me at odds with itself. For example, I want to buy all the clothes, that's part of 100% the game for me. The best way to earn money is to consistently get 20 or more wins in the battle zones. The best way to get 20 wins each night is to 1-shot kill everything. Well, when you can 1-shot everything, you are no longer engaging with the battle system as it was intended. So I didn't really get to experience it being cool, it was simple. I did it to myself, but this is a grind already, if I hadn't done that, it would have been an even bigger grind later. Another downer for me was the movement in the game. I knew we weren't going to have mounts like in PLA, and that amazing movement. But I was hoping for maybe an evolution throughout the game in the parkouring. Like at the very least let me jump over tiny fences. The justification for catching endless mons was weak in this game and not necessary. I get it, we liked it in PLA, but we didn't need it again. It just extended the grind for completing the side missions, again for my 100% completeness I want to do. Oh. The thing that actually bothered me the most. The battle victory screen is just 2 moments too long. It drove me nuts. Why wasn't it skippable? It feels like nitpicking at some point. I liked the game, I just didn't love it like I did PLA or even SV.

text: I actually agree about it having better aspects than arceus. People gotta look back on how a couple things in arceus are super hit or miss. Firstly, most blatant one, there is no weight to the story, because barely any character has any connections to you, or more dramatically even each other. Despite volo being probably the most important, the only one he's even connected to is cogita and everyone else doesn't really care. I think Z-A did such a good job with L not because he's a recurring character from XY but because his position in the story is like AZs position in XY with the added fact that he is an amnesiac genocidal maniac that everyone knows but the fact that zygarde picked him is really cool, like how volo and giratina work together except zygarde didn't get a major role till now I also think this game took a much better route and gave us mostly new characters instead of old ones/connected characters. Obviously everyone and their mother said they want to see clemont and other characters, which definitely should've been the DLCs job, but the new characters are actually really good? Especially compared to previous entries where all the characters almost never interacted with each other, and even in arceus barely anyone interacts with each other except adaman and irida. I also think they act really well as a replacement to gym leaders in a traditional sense, something which arceus was missing Another huge thing is Z-A made battling actually fun. I think arceus definitely went out of its way to provide tougher situations for the player, like pokemon ganging up on you and forcing set mode, but the battling isn't really enjoyable enough for me to point it out. Z-As battling is new and messy but it is genuinely very refreshing and the rogue mega battles work infinitely better than the battles in between the nobles fights, and a lot of arceus battles consisted of just one-shot moves which felt awkward I think arceus still provided a lot with its travelling (ride pokemon especially) items and zones but Z-A has a lot of really clean decisions that weren't present in arceus at all Also Z-A barely repeats mistakes of what sword and shield and scarlet violet made, which is refreshing to me, even though those 2 games are much more offering with the regions they have

text: Because every other aspect of the game other than the core gameplay loop just isn't good. And even the core gameplay loop is worse than its predecessor. The graphics are obviously bad, and the reused 2D maps of balconies and shit everywhere is embarrassing. The map is extremely small and lacks content. Normally it's one or the other, but in ZA it's both. Instead of having proper animations, all of the characters either have reused basic Fortnite emotes or the screen fades to imply that something happened. The day and night cycles are way too frequent to justify the unskippable cutscenes every single time the time of day transitions. The pathing of the pokemon is terrible. Before the update, you could only hold up to 999 shards at once, which is terrible considering how many mega stones there are and a lot of them cost 360. That's not enough to even by 3 at a time of the most expensive ones. And the game does not notify you when you've reached the max amount. You just have to figure it out the first time you spend forever farming and check your inventory to realize you just wasted a whole bunch of time. Despite being a sequal to legends Arceus, it didn't improve or iterate off of it in any way other than different battle mechanics. You'd expect a sequal to be better, or at least more polished, but nope. In fact, it's worse in some ways. Like there's no link cable, the wild zones are way worse than the open areas you could capture pokemon in before, the research tasks are worse, and the game doesn't look or play any better. That is just some surface level reasons as to why the game has a 'bad rap.'

text: Ok, so here's my take: Only really buy it if (1) you're a completionist or (2) you really like to shiny hunt pokemon, or there is a pokemon you like in the DLC that you want to shiny hunt and would be willing to pay $30 for it. Shiny hunting in this DLC, is probably the best out of all the games. I've completed the DLC, and it is definitely not worth $30, like maybe $15 at least. If you have the spare $30, and you enjoyed the game enough that you want to play more, then by all means go for it. The DLC story is honestly pretty lackluster. Takes place after everything in base-game (canonically after doing the Infinite ZA Royale 15 times iirc), 2 new characters, you and the gang check out this new phenomenon happening in Lumoise, yada yada repeat this same thing until the end of the story DLC. To put into perspective, imagine going from ZA-royale and grinding it until ranking up, except that there is little to no story in between each rank up. After that, it's shiny and legendary hunting time (though you can definitely do shiny hunting before then, but it's most effective at the end). Personally, I really enjoyed the base game enough to where I just said screw it and bought the DLC. Was the DLC bad? Not terribly, but the best way to explain it was 'did they even playtest their own game? Because wow they didn't think this through'. The reason for the mixed reviews is because the grindiness of the DLC can be really frustrating, as youre put onto a short time-limit and have to grind constantly for resources. I don’t regret purchasing the DLC though because I’ve been obsessed with this game (and still am). Despite this, in terms of actual worth it’s definitely not worth the $30 lol. Edit: Also, the chances of the DLC ever going on sale is really unlikely, I don't think they've ever put out a discount for pokemon DLCs ever? The closest you could get is buying gift cards, I know that you can get $100 nintendo gift cards for $90 at costco, and you can buy $10 nintendo gift cards for $9 on Amazon so at least you could pick up the dlc for $27 instead of $30 lol

text: I posted this in another subreddit and wanted to share my overall thoughts regarding the game. Reason to buy: I went into the game having not played a Pokemon game since Red. I really enjoyed Red, but I was also like 8 years old. I wanted to see what a 3D Pokemon game running at 60 fps felt like and if the writing and mechanics could win me over despite average or lackluster reviews (as a now 24 year old). Play time: 25 hours Pros: The game has that initial charm and the actual models for the Pokemon were pretty solid. The performance is good the 60 fps is maintained consistently. The fashion in game is solid as there are tons of options to make your character unique. The music is good, but a knock to that is when you are exploring Lumiose the soundtrack generally sounds the same. If 70% of your time is spent running around Lumiose exploring you are getting flooded with that track for majority of the game. So, although a good soundtrack, it can become annoying pretty quickly. Cons: The battles were not very challenging (especially if you utilize your EXP candy/Rare Candy. Most of my Pokemon were around lvl. 60 at rank D and I was running through teams. I did not really feel forced to take advantage of elemental weaknesses so therefore I didn't really learn them aside from the obvious ones. The writing was a bit too childish for me (the entire Canari section was a slog), and the fight 3 rogue mega evolving Pokemon, earn challenger ticket, and win promotional match got cumbersome after 3 cycles through it. Knowing I had to do it 2 more times to finish the game I knew that my time with the game was coming to a close. Overall Rating: C I am curious to hear what you all have to say! I truthfully want to hear opinions of those who actually gave the game a chance (not played for 3 hours and said it was trash).
label: spitting facts

text: I’m about 25 hours into the game and this is the first time I can say that I’ve put down a new Pokemon release and had to play another game to cleanse my palate midway through. At first, the battle system was fun and innovative, but after a little bit of time, the world felt uninspired and much less magical than I recall of the last few Pokemon games… and I was one of the few people who loved S&V despite their performance issues at launch (maybe I’m just getting older and don’t have the patience I used to). I think the big reason I put the game down was that the mega-evolution boss fights were a slog to get through. ACT 3 was boring and tedious and made the entire rest of the cast look entirely incompetent. That’s not to say this game’s bad. The core gameplay loop is good. They’re clearly trying something new within the strict confines of a series with a lot at stake, but it feels like a downgrade from even Arceus, where there were thematic elements that made the game that much more immersive (not to mention catching Pokemon is that much more of a PITA in this game). If the releases were switched around and this came before Arceus, I think I’d have a much fonder opinion of it.
label: spitting facts

text: Game was far too easy throughout and the amount of handholding was super frustrating, especially at the beginning when you just want to explore the world. Wayyyyy too much dialog, and the core loop staled quickly. I was occasionally over leveled despite constantly cycling my team to complete the dex. Rarely leveled anyone past high 50s or 60 or their highest level to learn a move. Unfortunately this just isn't meant to be an exploration or discovery game. The wild zones are small and never really have to be revisited after 1-2 visits to exhaust them. The side quests are largely fast-travel fetch quests with a lot of dialog. There are only a few meaningful shops, then pokemon centers, and A LOT of clothing stores. Cafes don't do much. You can't go into practically any of the buildings. The majority of the difficulty is spent on how to get on top of certain rooftops, though the scaffolding puzzles weren't very interesting. I was surprised at how short the game is. Whereas Arceus was for me an 80-100 hour game, this is looking more like 30-40 hours to 100% + however much time you want to spend on the ongoing online stuff. The battle system kinda seems like where they put the majority of the effort. Some mechanics still don't seem very relevant in this system e.g. most status or non-damaging moves, and often reduced to what you can 1 or 2 shot with type advantage. I'm glad they are trying something different from turn-based combat, but really hope the next game allows for more tactical battling somehow. Battle zones were fun though it was never particularly hard to earn enough points. Was sad to learn that climbing the ladder was so tied to the story progression in a way that made the ladder feel artificial. The ranked online is a ton of fun as are the 6 vs 6 private battles with friends. I plan to continue playing ranked especially if they keep it feeling fresh with new formats.
label: spitting facts

text: I've arrived at post-game and have like 9 or so sidequests open and like 190 or so in my PokeDex with about 50 hours spent. I did a bit of online battling to get the megastone. I liked the older protagonist and writing; it's a big step up from the last games and most of it was either charming, cute, silly or all of these combined. I liked the characters and their expressions - they are very pronounced but it fit nicely into the setting. Soundtrack is great. If you think it's too childish (which is absolutely a fair critique), games like Sword and Shield or Scarlet / Violet would drive you up the walls, the protagonist is younger there and so is the target audience for the writing. Only the Team Flare stuff I disliked a bit in some parts, because I thought they were framing stuff very weirdly. The city I enjoyed way more than I thought. It is a bit cramped and samey, on the other hand it feels alive and full, not devoid and empty like the last few entries. I didn't get bored of it at all and the indoor areas / dungeons were super cool, but a bit too short and there should've been more in general. The QoL improvements are great and I enjoyed them. It was weird that the Megastone shards or what they are called are capped at 999, because you need a lot of them and I didn't realise that there was a cap for a while... :D Battlesystem is a mixed bag I'd say. Overall it was a great experience because it feels like how I pictured it as a kid playing Pokemon Red and as they portrayed it in the anime back then. I also felt like status effects have a way bigger impact for casual play compared to other Pokemon games. Despite that, it's a bit clunky to use, especially switching between using moves and dodging and positioning your Pokemon properly. I feel like if they flesh that out a bit more, they can also tune fights a bit more tightly / move further in the direction they touched here, with stuff to dodge and so on. So, I would love to see them further improve and refine it, I think it has a lot of potential. I didn't use any candies and the level up stuff only for Pokemon from the bank, to level them up for quests, evolution, or whatever, never on the main team.The fights felt harder and more interesting than any other Pokemon in a loooong time, but I still wish there was more of it in general or at least more challenging post-game content - I think difficulty was fine for the main story. Maybe it needed a longer CD on item usage in fight. Sidequests were fun, varied and cute - biggest complain here is that they are basically ALL short, there should've been ones with longer sequences. But a lot of them were quirky and I enjoyed that. Overall I really enjoyed it, it has some shortcomings and I wish there was, well, just more of it, more indoor areas, etc. For me personally it was the best Pokemon game in the last 10+ years. If they improve on the formula in the next title and realise some of the untapped potential they still have there, it would be really great.

text: It's fun. In 4k, 60 fps and without the jagged lines everywhere, this art style looks good to me. Really clean, textures aren't the best but it's very pleasant to look at. It has a more modern, 'sheen' look to it compared to Scarlet's more earthy and nature vibe. It also juxtaposes really well to Arceus, just in general. Past VS future, catching VS battling type beat. I appreciate it as the next installment in the legends series, they could've done 'Legends Johto: Celebi' in the ancient Johto past, completely justifiably, but did something different instead Arceus was in the past, all about nature and mostly about catching. This game feels futuristic, and instead it's mostly about battling. It's about climbing the ranks which feels more relatable to an online ranked experience one would have in any multiplayer game they play VS the arbitrary 8 gyms, which while not bad, I like this change. I like the loop. You can just walk around and do whatever you want at any time. Night falls at a certain point, at which point it's advantageous to go battling and get the card to challenge the next gym leader. Other than that, the game is fully open world, and you can be motivated by whatever. I'm currently just trying to get money to get all my drip. Some really solid drip options in this game so I'm just trying to get rich while climbing up the ladder. Music is INCREDIBLE. An absolute treat. Probably the best part. I appreciate that traversing the city naturally gives you fast travel points. This combined with the gameplay loop made the experience feel like I was going to wherever I wanted at any time instead of progressing through a set path, which even Scarlet/Violet didn't do like this. The Metroidvania sandwich abilities kinda made you follow a path in Violet, but here anywhere you go is progress cause you unlock fast travel points and during the day, it doesn't really matter where you are. There's also some nice details. City feels alive, little kids running with thier Pokemon on the streets. NPCs feel a little mean, 'Nobody asked you!' , 'Why'd you come talk to me if you weren't gonna buy anything' which, as someone who's lived in NYC many years, I feel is 100% intentional. Just adding to the big city atmosphere, if you've lived in one you know how strangers are like. Overall a really solid game. Combat system is a downgrade, my only real complaint, but as a separate thing, it's fine. If the mainlime games adopt this battle system I'll be sad, but since Scarlet/Violet didn't implement Arceus' changes + Champions is coming out which is the classic combat system I think they won't.
label: spitting facts

text: I genuinely don't know what people think when they say that the city feels alive. Like did they play a completely different game? Did they buy the wrong game and thought it was ZA or something? Also don't forget that this quality is all coming from the MOST VALUABLE MEDIA FRANCHISE IN THE ENTIRE WORLD. After all this, Pokémon fans will still have the courage to say that the city feels alive and that the game looks good, it's just baffling to me how many people on this subreddit have the critical thinking of an impaired monkey.

text: Honestly yeah, I’m with you on this. It’s crazy how people keep justifying paying this much for a game that’s honestly mid at best, while just ignoring everything Nintendo could’ve done to make it better, all because “they’re having fun.” Like, sure, sometimes that's all that matters, and I had a decent time with it too, . But at this point, it’s starting to feel less like genuine enjoyment and more like people making excuses for a company that just wants to cash out again. And yeah, not every game needs top-tier graphics to be good, but come on , this one’s working with a smaller environment centered around a whole city based on Paris, yet somehow it feels like I’m walking through a ghost town. How about actually making the city feel alive? Give us something that feels lived-in, let us explore buildings again like in the older games, use more than just 2D flat textures for once. Then there’s the DLC mess and the half-baked megas (not all of them, but still). I just can’t recommend anyone spend full price on this. At best, this feels like a $35–$40 game. And the whole “it’s just a spin-off” argument is laughable too. I’ve played way better spin-offs in other series. Hell, even Let’s Go had way more charm and personality, Its charm and style made it feel like a game with actual soul. Call me nitpicky or whatever, and you’re free to enjoy it how you want. But don’t sit here and act like Nintendo or Game Freak can’t do better. They’ve proven plenty of times that they absolutely can, they just choose not to.

text: I can’t give it anything higher than a 6/10. It’s the most visually impressive Pokémon game since leaving behind the route-based design, and the final fight was very cinematic, which was cool. I liked the new battle system. The music was top notch. The day-to-day flow kept a good pace going. There were some fun characters in the mix and the dialogue had some actual humor to it. But I thought the story was a bit of a mess, especially leading up to the climax, and it was disappointingly repetitive, which felt a little lazy. The map itself felt decently constructed but there weren’t enough interiors for me, and I wish they had done more with the design of the wild zones and sewers to help diversify the feel of things. There was an overabundwnce of side quests that felt meaningless and too few that felt like they actually brought depth to the setting or story, so it was hard to feel connected to Lumiose. All in all, it felt like a game with good bones but not nearly enough meat to it, which is surprising because the game was delayed by a full year. The fact that you can’t interact with your Pokémon at all aside from taking pictures together is so strange, because you can tap A to interact with Pokémon in the overworld - it’s that kind of weird oversight that made the game feel half-baked overall. That said, there were still some pretty big strides in there, so I’m hopeful Wind & Waves will feel like a more feature-complete experience, because if they run as well as Z-A did and have those cool cinematic moments, then they have a good shot at being great games.

text: I actually think 7/10 is very fair and feels about right to me. At first the monotonous look of Lumois City and the Wild Zones threw me off but after a while I got used to it. Underneath the scathing reviews and window texture memes, there really is a genuine Pokémon experience. My biggest gripe was the amount of hours the game keeps you on rails, with the excessive hand holding Taunie/Urbain telling you what to do. It takes way too many hours for the game to open up. Also I think it’s fair to demand a lot more in terms of graphics and variants in content from one of, if not the biggest IPs on earth. The game definitely put effort in specific areas but cut corners on a lot. As for the DLC, I had a similar initial bad impression thinking this gameplay loop is no good. However, after some research and the snowballing of resources, this also becomes not a huge issue as well. Legends ZA is slop that I can turn on if I want some downtime. My 6 year old however thinks this game is the best game on earth though, so yeah kids calling it 10/10 is spot on.

text: It’s not bad as a whole, it just has a lot working against it. The story is good (great if you loved XY, as I do), the characters are interesting visually and narratively, the real time battle system is super engaging compared to the traditional style, the gameplay loop is fun (for a while, at least), and the city is more interesting than people expected. Conversely, the city is objectively not that interesting and is potentially the most visually repetitive Pokemon setting yet, the environments are held back by obvious graphics shortfalls, the cutscenes are written and presented in such a way that they almost demand voice acting which, of course, they lack, and the gameplay loop of “rack up points to earn a ticket” gets old after a while. The postgame turned me off immediately because it seemed that I was going to have to do that loop a million times just to get some narrative closure, and by then it had already exhausted me. The DLC also seems to be built around an endless grind, so there was no chance I’d purchase it. If you love Pokemon, you’ll enjoy yourself. 7/10 is where I’d score it also. It’s a nice exploration of new concepts that I hope stick around, but it’s not one I’ll play through a second time. Arceus, on the other hand, I’m down to play through every year or two. If they take what worked from both of these games and use it for a legends Unova or Johto, I’d be over the moon. Also, and this is not a Pokemon-specific issue, but overpriced DLC announced pre-launch is SUCH a gaudy practice and I’d love for devs and publishers to at least pretend they’re shipping a complete game from the jump. Gating megas behind the DLC left a bad taste in my mouth I guess.
label: spitting facts

text: On the switch I've played Shield, Scarlet, LA, and ZA so far, and I think ZA is pretty decent. The map itself isn't that great, but the actual selling point of ZA is the battle system. The graphics and movement during battle are clean, and the game itself is a ton more immersive (personal preference). I feel like the way that moves and battles work in ZA alone make it worth it, and the Mega Evos are just the cherry on top. During the playthrough, I had 5 out of 6 pkmn on my team equipped with a Mega Stone, so I got tons of the megas, and I love them. I think that the battles were easy enough while simultaneously being slightly challenging (if you decide not to tryhard and overlevel, which is actually really easy in this game). I think that both ZA and LA are definitely worth it, and if you really want the Mega Evolution then go for ZA. It is definitely not as bad as people are making it out to be. If you prefer looking at and exploring the world and you prefer the story over the battles, Legends Arceus would be a better choice. But I've enjoyed battles the most in Legends ZA. The battle system itself is a bit hard to get used to, but it is definitely less repetitive and more immersive. If you're willing to shell out for the DLC, the shiny pokemon come a lot easier in ZA as well. I still have a couple criticisms for ZA though. One is obviously that you can't really move around outside Lumiose, but surprisingly, I've had only a couple issues with that. There is enough content to keep you entertained for 100+ hours within the city. My biggest issue is how handholdy it is. The quests are always "Do this, do that, beat these guys", and that kinda ruins immersion. The game also pretends to give you a choice (Like joining the Rust Syndicate or leaving Lumiose) and then decides that no, you don't have that choice. Overall, I've been loving my playthrough of ZA, mostly for the battles and the megas. The plot itself is kinda trash though.

text: Yesterday I finished the main quest and all the side quests. I’m not completely biased: I didn’t like Sword/Shield or Scarlet/Violet, I could list my reasons but this post isn’t about that. This is the best Pokémon game I’ve played in years and easily one of my top 3 games in the entire saga if not my favorite. Time will tell. The characters are charismatic again, I loved every single one of them. The combat system was refreshing, this is how I understand Pokémon in 2025 and I wouldn’t mind if they keep polishing it and keep it for future games. The story is amazing, I feel like Legends are taking what wasn’t fully adressed in previous games and develop it to provide a closure, this felt like XY-2. Every unsolved question has now an answer. The game is great overall but the last hours of gameplay are incredible. They took everything that worked in Arceus and elevated to a whole new level. I was so happy as a 35yo fan who has been playing since 98 I had a lil tears in my eyes after the final credits. I hope they deliver an awesome DLC with more story and megas, and maybe we can have a few mechanics back (breeding or 1vs1 battles). The online battles are chaotic and liked them but I think 1vs1 has more potential. It’s a shame people on the Internet are super angry about balconies and buildings, maybe they should play SimCity or start an architecture degree because that has 0 impact on fun. I’m also tired of people trying to force us Digimon games and the comparisons. They both provide very different experiences and the comparisons are just dumb. Digimon also have its flaws like corridor-like and poor designed levels maybe they should focus on their game’s own flaws or simply enjoy it and quit the Pokémon hate train already. Nothing’s going to replace Pokémon no matter how hard they try and it’s annoying atp. Pokémon looks and plays better than ever, and no metacritic review bombing or hate waves on the Internet by angry people can take that away from me. Is there room for improvement? Of course there is. But it has little to do with what they say. I hope they keep on doing the formula, improving things and giving us games this good. Legends ZA is, in my opinion, a massive step in the right direction. I hope y’all enjoy it as much as I’m still doing <3

text: I know this place is just an echo chamber and people here ARE biased even when they say they aren’t but here’s my take: I’m a huge Pokemon fan since day 1 and I think the gameplay in this game is fantastic (although we need more ways to use our Pokemon: flight etc) The story is solid (though I found it short) The characters are great. I can forgive all of that, but the post game doesn't interest me at all. Two legendaries to catch (and they're absurdly easy), you've probably very easily caught everything in the wild zones already, and an infinite grind doesn't seem quite as fun when it's pointless (especially once you realize it's literally just the same opponents again, at least at first - why is my first post game fight against LEVEL TEN Pokemon?!) [Edit: I realized after posting that only the first opponent in the infinite grind is an original/low level one, the next one is more suitable - I still stand by my point that it's kind of a boring endgame though. The gameplay loop seems like it would be a fun part of a game, but not an entire game by itself.] I'm happy some people are liking the game, and I certainly didn't hate it, but I think my problem is less the result and more that it just feels lazy. I know someone is going to combat this by saying “you shouldn’t have to play more to enjoy the game” which is fair but also I don’t think they should give people every ability as soon as the game starts as that’s just a part of progressing. I never felt like there was not enough content. There was never a point in the game that I wasn’t sure what I should do, I got around 25-30 hours before beating the game and that’s without replayability with challenge runs, shiny hunting, online battles (which I got rank A in and thought was very fun), and future dlc. text: There are a lot of reasons to dislike this game. On-Rails: The first couple of hours of gameplay are strictly on-rails. You can’t go that way. Or that way. Or that way. FOLLOW EXACTLY THIS PATH AND NOTHING ELSE!!! It’s a Pokémon game so we expect some directional leading, but this was ridiculous! Starters: Unlike PLA that had 3 starters from 3 different regions, and other Pokémon games that had all new starters, this has an extremely lopsided choice of 2 starters from one generation and 1 from another. It’s completely odd and doesn’t make any sense. No New Pokémon: Nothing new. No new Pokémon. No new forms. Just new Mega’s. And considering every generation has a new system from Shadow Pokémon, to Mega’s, to Z-Moves, to Dynamax, to Terra Crystals, none of it matters past the current generation. So even if you collect all of the Mega’s, it won’t matter past this game. Yes, every generation has its own system, but they are always offset by having new Pokémon and/or new regional forms. Not here. Size: The map is ridiculously small. It’s like the development team said, “How small and insignificant of a game can we make without losing our entire player base? Our fans will devour whatever crap we make in spite of it, so why put the effort in?” The Pokémon Legends Name: PLA was an absolute masterpiece! Could it have been better? Probably. But it gave us the world we all wanted. It was a tribute to the roots of Pokemon, and made you feel like you truly were exploring a whole new, yet familiar world. You were creating the very first Pokédex, and what you did mattered. Then this slop came along with the Pokémon Legends name and it was a slap in the face. Price: The Switch 1 version was one of the most expensive games released on the Switch. More expensive than the mainline SwSh and SV. Yet what we got was this absolute slop. Then you have the DLC that released an insultingly short time after the initial release. It’s pretty clear now why. Add to that the price increase for the DLC with a clear decrease in quality of work and quality & quantity of content. Then there’s all the little stuff, like the lack of details, and the poor online matchmaking, and patching out the overfill shiny hunting method, and the lack of ongoing content that we got in the mainline games that were less expensive, etc., etc., etc. Battle System: The only real redeeming quality is the new battle system. You can tell that a lot of time and energy was put into making it right. It works, and it works well.
label: spitting facts

text: I also love it, but there are definite flaws. Building detail for one. They LOOK nice, but they could at least have used some normal mapping. They are flat, and lack any real depth. Just a simple Normal Map would of helped, but I'd like to see this improved in the next games to see a little more depth in the environment. Environement is another. You're locked entirely to Lumiose. While again, it looks good, everywhere looks pretty samey, which is a stark contrast to almost every other pokemon game in existence. Sure, there's a FEW areas that look different, which is nice, but it's mostly dressing on an otherwise very samey world. Being able to actually interact with our Pokemon. It's a nice step forward that we can now run around with them and have them do basic things with us in the game world, but now we need actual interaction. The photo mode needs work. It's VERY restrictive in where you can move the camera, and having Pokemon always facing directly at the camera when you play the music, is infuriating. I want them doing an action but not necessarily facing the camera!!! More variations of colours for the clothing. Hell, just give us a colour wheel and some colour channels to manipulate, then just give us a bunch of different clothing styles and patterns to play with! Pokemon customization, as in, accessorizing our pokes. Just simply things like bows, bracelets, necklaces, stuff that could just be resized and given anchor points on most pokes. Actually make the whole body type thing come into play even. Those are my main gripes really. I know a lot of people criticize the battle system because there's no abilities, and there's some insane powercreep when it comes to pokemon stats, but lets be real... if a pokemon REQUIRES it's ability to even function on a basic level, something else is an issue. I quite like the more free, RPG-esc, real time battle system. It makes you feel like you're in an actual battle and not just "You go, ok my turn, ok now you go", which is super artificial and really doesn't drive home that these are BATTLES with ANIMALS. The way Legends Z-A does it feels similar to how it's represented in the Anime, which is how I always wanted Pokemon battles to be like.

text: I think critisism doesn’t equate to hate imo, theres a lot people are disappointed about in Z-A; such as the environment being only about the size of the great plateau in breath of the wild. That is so little seeing as it is supposed to be an open world game. I was really looking forward to exploring the catacombs aswell, they made it seem like it was gonna be this huge thing but then it ended up being a very tiny few areas that weren’t even that long. Other than that there’s a couple things that may seem smaller but still had a huge impact on the way i like to play games. The music (while pretty cool in some select few trainer battles) is largely one single track that keeps looping in the overworld and it got to the point I had to turn off my volume because it was so egregious. The tutorial is right around an hour long and greatly tanks the replayability of the game. Nothing actually interesting happens on the map while you’re playing, npc’s move statically back and forth sometimes and not even the overworld pokemon have anything to do other than stand in their respective spawn spots. Buildings are largely the same exact model and size of one another with slight differences in scaling and color apart from some outliers and you can only access around 10-14 of the houses within the ENTIRE city. Isn’t ZA supposed to be a city exploration type game? Not to mention some buildings straight up float 2 inches off the ground. The battle system, while fun for a couple hours at best, is rather redundant once you figure out that there is no strategizing to do anymore because the best way to win every fight is standing still and button mashing. Many people may be tired of hearing this but i am unfortunately also annoyed by the lack of voice acting, it gets to a point where cutszenes are barely readable without squinting for any head motions to indicate speech otherwise you won’t know who’s talking (prime example is literally the first cutszene with the tower) You can walk across the entire map within just around 3 minutes and around the map in about 10. By comparison it would take hours to get around the breath of the wild map. Those are criticisms i personally have, because a game that costs 70$ should not be struggling with this many aspects and i feel kind of saddened with how much I hoped this game would be. People who criticise ZA are often pokemon fans themselves and we’re not trying to take your fun away from your experience. We just expected more out of Pokemon for this release, please continue to enjoy your game because its within your right to do so (the game is 70$ man im glad some people are having a good experience with it at least!)

At no point during the ZA playthrough did it actually feel like my character was particularly free to do what I wanted. Plus, maybe 5 of the 20 wild zones even felt like they thematically fit the pokemon found there. Just horrible environment design. Then I got to the basegame post-game, freedom finally obtained...and it just felt empty and boring. I wouldn't say it was bad, but the way I initially thought about it still holds true for me. There are Pokemon games I loved and a few elements of previous Pokemon games I hated. But ZA's post-game just made me feel nothing. Absolutely nothing. None of the tasks felt particularly fun to do. There were something like 100 side quests? Maybe 5 of those were even remotely interesting. Only a few new pokemon in Wild Zone 20, extremely repetitive battles, many of which were against teams I had already fought and still needed to fight over and over and over again to eventually get the shiny charm. It really just felt like I was playing to get the Floette and shiny charm and there was no real depth to anything along the way. Then the DLC came out...that tedious hellscape. That's where I went from feeling nothing to feeling annoyed. The worst Pokemon experience in 30 years of games. I can totally understand people playing and enjoying the main story part of the basegame, but I tend to really remember Pokemon games for the stuff after the main story...especially the DLC with the recent games where the DLC significantly improved both Sword/Shield and Scarlet/Violet. The DLC actually made ZA an even worse experience. It was empty and boring without the DLC but now it's just actually a bad game.

text: I’ve seen a ton of posts lately saying things like, “I don’t get the hate — I had a great time with Legends Z-A.” And honestly? Same. Most of us did. That’s not the issue. No one’s saying Legends Z-A isn’t fun. It’s still Pokémon — charming, nostalgic, and easy to sink hours into. The frustration isn’t about enjoyment. It’s about the fact that one of the richest gaming franchises in the world keeps putting out games that barely meet modern quality standards. Most of the people criticizing it are the same fans who buy every Pokémon release. We’re not “haters.” We’re just tired of seeing a billion-dollar IP continually cut corners while the cost of gaming keeps climbing. We’re in an era where game prices are up, DLC is standard, and even Nintendo’s hardware is more expensive — yet Pokémon still feels like it’s standing still. It’s not unreasonable to expect a series this successful to raise its standards with the rest of the industry. Enjoying something and wanting it to improve aren’t contradictions. They come from the same place — caring enough to expect better.

text: Legends Arceus had large areas to explore with good variety. PLA also had its ride pokemon and they were well implemented. Jumping from a Wyrdeer to a Braviary is very satisfying. It was fast and fluid to navigate the different locations. You had flight, swimming, and climbing. ZA has wildzones. While I do like them, they are quite small. I would like something to break up the sameness of Lumiose. An expanded sewer would have been nice. Imagine if it stretched across the entirety of the Lumiose underground and it doubled as a quick way to get around? Places like the old Team Flare lab are a step in the right direction. It’s a favorite of mine. I miss the Arceus ball mechanics. Feather balls could snipe far away pokemon and the gigaton balls had a short arc requiring a close throw. The sticky globs were cool too. You really felt like you were in the fight, not just your pokemon.

text: As someone who has spent 300 hours in the game, the biggest gripe I have about the base game is the world: The world doesn't feel 'believably real' so I was never able to fully immerse into it. No way around it, the building walls being flat images copy pasted everywhere makes it feel very 'fake'. Like a low budget, student movie set (maybe even worse). While I don't mind that the entire game being in 1 city, the city lacks variation. You see a handful of building variations, all the cafes look the same, most of the streets look almost the same. A real city would have more character. And the worst offender are the NPCs. They mostly just stand around, you might see a couple people jogging around, some sitting on benches but that's it. Compare this to any other modern game like Baldur's Gate 3, Cyber Punk 2077, or even older games like Skyrim, Witcher 3 and you see a huge difference. None of the NPCs are living lives, they stand at the same spot 24/7, there are no cars on the road, no one's biking, no one's going walking home after doing their shopping, the NPCs in the battle areas (the tennis court looking things, not battle zones) aren't even battling, they just stand around with their pokemon facing each other. When you finish some NPC's sidequests, they just stand around... for eternity. All those reasons make me hate walking around in the city. It just painfully reminds me that 'I'm playing a video game', not 'I'm exploring Lumiose!'. I have a lot more complains about the DLC, but I'll save that for another day.
label: spitting facts

text: I love LZ, but it’s far from perfect. For almost everything it does right it’s a step forward with a step backwards or at minimum sideways. good verticality and decent size map Small map relative to recent releases, and really a single locale +wild zones/wormholes are a cool concept small, limited variety of environments fun battle mechanics huge step back in terms of movement/traversal from S/V and L:A no ride pokemon? Limited fast travel options last chance/being able to catch a pokemon after knocking it out capture mechanics feel like a step back from L:A in every other way no home support and one way travel when it does happen (which would probably be fine if they hadn’t shown it’s entirely possible with LG, S/S, S/V and L:A time limits on battle zones/wormholes -grindy/repetitive with the battle zones I vaguely recall an interview from a few years back that the developers intentionally don’t bring back all well-received features beyond the core mechanics because they want each generation to have a unique experience. This feels like a case where that design philosophy led to a fun but deeply flawed experience
label: spitting facts
